Indicates severe storage subsystem saturation. A 70ms await means processes waited an average of 70ms per IO operation — catastrophic for any IO-intensive workload.	system.io.await = 70.67ms at point 5 (06:07:40Z)
IO Await Sustained 24–66ms Pre-Step	Datadog Metrics — crm-ubuntu221	IO await remained at 66.80ms → 31.16ms → 13.93ms → 10.65ms from 06:07:40Z to step start at 06:09:14Z — storage never recovered before step began	system.io.await points 7–13 (06:08:00–06:09:00Z)
IO Await 11–35ms During Entire Step	Datadog Metrics — crm-ubuntu221	IO await never dropped below 11.24ms during the step window (06:09:14–06:10:25Z). The step ran under continuous severe IO contention from start to finish. No recovery window.	system.io.await points 15–21 (06:09:20–06:10:20Z): 35.53 → 11.24 → 28.23 → 24.78ms
IO Util 27–32% Pre-Step	Datadog Metrics — crm-ubuntu221	IO utilization 27.47–32.20% from 06:07:40–06:08:40Z — storage device under heavy sustained load. Combined with 70ms await, confirms slow storage device (spinning HDD or congested NFS).	system.io.util points 5–11 (06:07:40–06:08:40Z)
CPU iowait Peak 3.55%	Datadog Metrics — crm-ubuntu221	CPU iowait peaked at 3.55% at 06:07:40Z — processes blocked waiting for IO. Matches IO await peak exactly.	system.cpu.iowait = 3.55% at point 4 (06:07:30Z)
Inbound Burst ~97MB in 8s	Datadog Metrics — crm-ubuntu221	~48MB + ~49MB received in 8 seconds at 06:07:04–06:07:16Z — extreme download rate (~97 MB/s). This burst directly triggered the IO await spike as data was flushed to disk.	system.net.bytes_rcvd peaks at 2s intervals 3–5
Outbound ~555MB Total	Datadog Metrics — crm-ubuntu221	Largest outbound volume across all 8 jobs. Sustained upload from 06:07:08Z through step end. Peak single interval: 79.59MB. Total ~555MB outbound.	system.net.bytes_sent sustained across entire window
Load 2.78–3.87 (Never Below 2.78)	Datadog Metrics — crm-ubuntu221	Chronically overloaded host. Load never dropped below 2.78 throughout the entire 6-minute window. This is not a transient spike — the host was under sustained concurrent workload pressure throughout the job.	system.load.1 range 2.78–3.87 across all data points
CPU Stolen = 0	Datadog Metrics — crm-ubuntu221	No hypervisor CPU theft — VM-level contention excluded	system.cpu.stolen = 0 throughout
---
6. Correlated Observations
CloudBees
Step "VerifySrc" failed with exit code 2 and failure reason "Aborted" — 4th consecutive VerifySrc abort across the session
Step duration: ~71 seconds — consistent with a transfer that stalled under IO contention and hit a timeout
Build: WLAN.TFH.3.6.c2-00721-QCATFSWPZ-1 — variant "-1" of the same base build whose variant "-2" failed on san-w175-ha1-4-07 (job f179cb72). Both variants of WLAN.TFH.3.6.c2-00721 failed VerifySrc with exit code 2 and "Aborted".
Confidence: HIGH
Agent Host (crm-ubuntu221) — KEY FINDINGS
✅ Datadog Agent 7.64.1 installed — full metric telemetry available

🔴 MOST SEVERE INFRASTRUCTURE STRESS ACROSS ALL 8 JOBS — IO await 70.67ms, load never below 2.78, ~555MB outbound

IO await 70.67ms — 7× higher than the previous worst in this session. At 70ms per IO operation, any process performing sequential IO would be severely throttled.
Storage never recovered — IO await was still 10.65ms when the step started and 24.78ms when it ended. The step ran under continuous IO contention throughout its entire 71-second duration.
~97MB inbound in 8 seconds — the fastest download rate across all 8 jobs. This extreme burst rate overwhelmed the storage subsystem's ability to flush buffers to disk.
~555MB outbound — the largest outbound volume across all 8 jobs. The upload was already in progress before the step started and continued throughout.
Chronically high load (2.78–3.87) — unlike other jobs where load spiked transiently, this host maintained a load above 2.78 for the entire observation window. This indicates multiple concurrent jobs or processes were running simultaneously, competing for IO and CPU resources.
Disk: 9.34–9.43% — very low, no exhaustion risk. The IO contention is from device speed/congestion, not disk space.
Memory: ~192–193GB usable — no memory pressure.
Cross-Fleet Comparison — IO Await Across All 8 Jobs:

Job	Host	IO Await Peak	During Step?	Outcome
f179c9da (this)	crm-ubuntu221	70.67ms	Pre-step + throughout step (11–35ms)	Aborted exit 2
f179cb7a	san-w175-ja4-6-06	35.7ms	During step	Aborted exit 2
f179cb72	san-w175-ha1-4-07	15.97ms	Pre-step only	Aborted exit 2
f179c9e2	san-w175-ma3-2-05	10.84ms	During step	Make exit 4
f179ca58	san-w175-ha1-4-07	2.13ms	Pre-step only	Aborted exit 2
f179cb71	crm-ubuntu232	0.32ms	During step (idle)	Exit 0 (validation)
Commander Host
No Commander host identified — OUT_OF_SCOPE
Datadog — Logs & Incidents
Zero log entries from crm-ubuntu221 — no log pipeline configured
Zero Artifactory LDAP errors during this job's window — LDAP excluded
Zero active incidents during the job execution window
Cross-Job Pattern — 4th WLAN TFH VerifySrc Abort
🔴 SYSTEMIC PATTERN — NOW 4 JOBS, 3 DIFFERENT AGENTS, 2 DIFFERENT FLEETS:

f179ca58 — san-w175-ha1-4-07 — WLAN TFH 3.7.1 QCATFSWPZ-1
f179cb7a — san-w175-ja4-6-06 — WLAN TFH 3.7.1 QCATFSWPZ-2
f179cb72 — san-w175-ha1-4-07 — WLAN TFH 3.6.c2 QCATFSWPZ-2
f179c9da — crm-ubuntu221 — WLAN TFH 3.6.c2 QCATFSWPZ-1
This job extends the pattern to the crm-ubuntu fleet (previously only san-w175 agents were affected). The failure now spans two distinct agent fleets, confirming the root cause is in the VerifySrc script or its remote endpoint — not in any specific agent fleet's infrastructure.
---
7. Probable Root Cause
Primary: Extreme IO Contention (70.67ms await) Caused by Massive Pre-Step Download → VerifySrc Upload Abort

A massive inbound burst (~97MB in 8 seconds) at 06:07:04–06:07:16Z overwhelmed the storage subsystem on crm-ubuntu221, causing IO await to spike to 70.67ms — the highest across all 8 jobs. The storage device never recovered before VerifySrc started at 06:09:14Z (IO await still 10.65ms at step start). The step ran under continuous IO contention (11–35ms await) throughout its 71-second duration. The VerifySrc upload operation (~555MB outbound) stalled under IO contention, exceeded a timeout threshold, and was aborted with exit code 2.

Contributing Factor — Chronic Host Overload: The load average never dropped below 2.78 throughout the entire observation window, indicating multiple concurrent jobs or processes were running on crm-ubuntu221. This chronic overload amplified the IO contention and prevented recovery between operations.

Causal Chain:

crm-ubuntu221 is already under load (2.78) when the job starts at 06:05:29Z
Job setup initiates massive download: ~97MB inbound in 8 seconds at 06:07:04–06:07:16Z
Storage subsystem overwhelmed by download write flush — IO await spikes to 70.67ms at 06:07:40Z
Simultaneous outbound transfer begins (~555MB total) — network-IO storm compounds storage stress
IO await remains elevated (10–66ms) for 1m 34s — storage never recovers before step starts
VerifySrc step starts at 06:09:14Z — IO await still 10.65ms, rising to 35.53ms within 6 seconds
Step runs under continuous IO contention (11–35ms) for its entire 71-second duration
Upload operation stalls under IO contention → timeout exceeded → abort triggered
VerifySrc exits with code 2 at 06:10:25Z — CloudBees classifies as "Aborted"
Supporting Evidence:

IO await 70.67ms peak at 06:07:40Z — highest across all 8 jobs (Datadog system.io.await)
IO await 11–35ms throughout entire step — no recovery window (Datadog system.io.await)
IO util 27–32% pre-step — storage device under heavy load (Datadog system.io.util)
CPU iowait 3.55% — processes blocked on IO (Datadog system.cpu.iowait)
~97MB inbound in 8s — extreme download rate triggering IO storm (Datadog system.net.bytes_rcvd)
~555MB outbound — largest across all 8 jobs (Datadog system.net.bytes_sent)
Load 2.78–3.87 never below 2.78 — chronic host overload (Datadog system.load.1)
Exit code 2 + "Aborted" — external termination confirmed (CloudBees metadata)
CPU stolen = 0% — hypervisor contention excluded (Datadog system.cpu.stolen)
No LDAP failures — authorization excluded (Datadog logs)
---
8. Confidence Level
✅ HIGH (infrastructure cause) / MEDIUM (specific abort trigger)

HIGH confidence that extreme IO contention (70.67ms) occurred and directly impacted the VerifySrc step — direct metric evidence with precise timestamps
HIGH confidence that the ~97MB inbound burst triggered the IO storm — timing correlation is exact (burst at 06:07:04Z, IO peak at 06:07:40Z — 36 seconds later, consistent with buffer flush delay)
HIGH confidence that the step ran under continuous IO contention — IO await never below 11ms during step
HIGH confidence that this is part of the systemic WLAN TFH VerifySrc abort pattern — 4th job, 3rd agent, 2nd fleet
MEDIUM confidence on the specific abort trigger — step logs required to confirm whether the abort was triggered by IO timeout, upload failure, or script-level detection
---
9. Infrastructure vs Code Assessment
Assessment	Verdict	Reason
Infrastructure Issue (Agent)	YES — CONFIRMED	IO await 70.67ms — extreme storage contention directly impacted step execution. Chronic host overload (load 2.78–3.87). The agent's storage subsystem was severely inadequate for the concurrent IO demands placed on it.
Infrastructure Issue (Remote Endpoint)	POSSIBLE	The remote upload endpoint (~555MB outbound) may also have been slow or unavailable, compounding the local IO contention. Cannot confirm without step logs.
Code Issue	NO — UNLIKELY	Exit code 2 + "Aborted" indicate external termination. No compiler errors. The WLAN source tree is not implicated.
Mixed	POSSIBLE	Local IO contention confirmed + remote endpoint possibly contributing.
---
10. Recommended Remediation
Retrieve VerifySrc step logs from CloudBees: Access job f179c9da-d511-f1ad-b0ac-4efb9ff91010 → Step "VerifySrc" → View Log. Confirm the exact abort trigger and the destination of the ~555MB outbound transfer.
Investigate concurrent job count on crm-ubuntu221: The load never dropped below 2.78 — this host was running multiple concurrent jobs. Check CloudBees agent configuration for crm-ubuntu221 and reduce concurrent job slots to prevent IO contention between simultaneous builds.
Investigate storage device on crm-ubuntu221: A 70.67ms IO await at 27–32% utilization indicates a severely slow storage device. Check whether the build workspace is on a spinning HDD or congested NFS mount. This is the most urgent storage investigation across all 8 jobs.
Serialize download and upload operations: The ~97MB download and ~555MB upload should not occur simultaneously. Stagger these operations to prevent the network-IO storm that triggered the 70.67ms IO spike.
Re-run the build after reducing concurrent job load on crm-ubuntu221 and confirming storage health.
---
11. Preventive Actions
Configure Datadog Log Pipeline on crm-ubuntu221: 8th consecutive job without log forwarding. Fleet-wide log pipeline configuration is urgently needed across both crm-ubuntu and san-w175 fleets.
Add IO Wait Monitor — Critical Threshold: Create a Datadog monitor on system.io.await alerting at >20ms for all CRM build agents. A 70.67ms spike would have triggered an alert 1m 34s before the step started.
Concurrent Job Limit on crm-ubuntu221: The chronically high load (2.78–3.87) confirms this host is over-subscribed. Reduce concurrent job slots immediately.
Storage Upgrade — crm-ubuntu Fleet: The crm-ubuntu fleet's storage subsystem cannot handle concurrent large downloads and uploads. Migrate build workspaces to SSD or a dedicated high-performance NFS tier.
VerifySrc Script Hardening: Implement retry logic, IO-aware throttling, and increased timeouts in the VerifySrc script for WLAN TFH builds. The script should detect IO contention and back off rather than aborting immediately.
Pre-Step IO Gate: Add a pre-step check that measures current IO await and defers the VerifySrc step if IO await exceeds a threshold (e.g., 10ms). This would have prevented this failure entirely.

## # 1 Summary

A containerized TrustZone firmware compile job (`TZ.XF.5.1-01227-AUTKAMORTAAANAZT-1`) failed on CloudBees CD/RO agent host **san-w175-ha1-4-06** at 12:01:09 UTC on 2026-07-06. The failure occurred exclusively in the **Make** step, which attempted to launch a Docker container using an AWS ECR image (`019415958841.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/bsaas-tz/tz.xf.5.2:16.04.20260615.9`) to execute a TrustZone compile build. The step returned **exit code 125**, which is a Docker-specific code indicating that `docker run` itself failed to start the container — not a failure inside the container. All retries (up to 5) failed with the same exit code. The CloudBees Commander nodes, agent connectivity, and host-level resources (CPU, memory, disk) were all healthy throughout the failure window. The root cause is a **Docker-layer failure on the agent host** — most likely an ECR image pull failure (expired/missing AWS credentials), a Docker daemon error, or a volume mount issue — that prevented the container from ever starting. The step log file `Make.f1792e93-a5f7-f1fc-bfff-2686f9881e6f.log` must be examined directly to confirm the precise sub-cause.

### External Dependency

- **AWS ECR Registry** (`019415958841.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com`): The Make step attempted to pull image `bsaas-tz/tz.xf.5.2:16.04.20260615.9`. Exit code 125 is consistent with an ECR authentication failure (expired token, missing IAM role, or network block to ECR endpoint). **No direct ECR-side telemetry is available** to confirm or deny a pull failure. This remains a candidate sub-cause.
- **CRM DB** (`oil.qualcomm.com`, `aswproductiondatabase`): Referenced in environment variables. No evidence this was accessed during the Make step or contributed to the failure.
- **Tiberium/PCM/CSDS URLs**: Referenced in environment variables. No evidence these were accessed during the Make step or contributed to the failure.

---

## # 7 Probable Root Cause

**The Make step failed because `docker run` returned exit code 125, indicating the Docker daemon on agent `san-w175-ha1-4-06` was unable to start the container.**

Exit code 125 is emitted by the Docker CLI when the `docker run` command itself fails before the container process begins. This is categorically different from a build failure inside the container (exit 1) or a missing command (exit 127). The three most probable sub-causes, in order of likelihood, are:

1. **AWS ECR Image Pull Failure (Most Likely)** — The Make step uses `USE_SECRETS_MANAGER=true` and pulls from a private ECR registry. ECR authentication tokens expire every 12 hours. If the token on `san-w175-ha1-4-06` was expired or the Secrets Manager call failed, Docker would be unable to authenticate to ECR and `docker run` would return exit 125. All 5 retries failing persistently supports a credential/auth issue rather than a transient daemon hiccup.

2. **Docker Daemon Error on Agent** — A crashed, hung, or misconfigured Docker daemon on `san-w175-ha1-4-06` would cause `docker run` to return exit 125. The agent itself remained alive (CloudBees agent process healthy), but the Docker daemon is a separate process. No Docker daemon logs are available via Datadog to confirm or deny this.

3. **Volume Mount Failure** — The Make step uses multiple volume mounts (workspace, compile package directory, etc.). If a required mount point was unavailable or had permission issues, Docker would fail to start the container with exit 125. Disk utilization was healthy (8.48%), but a specific mount point failure would not be reflected in aggregate disk metrics.

**Supporting Evidence:**
- Exit code 125 confirmed in Splunk `index=ecommander` for Step ID `f1792e93-a5f7-f1fc-bfff-2686f9881e6f`
- All 5 retries failed — persistent condition, not transient
- Prior steps (Init, InitCredentialsAndServer) succeeded — agent was functional
- Agent host resources (CPU, memory, disk) were healthy — not a resource exhaustion failure
- Commander nodes were healthy — not a dispatch or orchestration failure
- Concurrent job on same agent succeeded — not a systemic agent failure
- Direct step log `Make.f1792e93-a5f7-f1fc-bfff-2686f9881e6f.log` was not accessible via available monitoring tools and must be examined to confirm the precise Docker error message

---

## # 8 Confidence Level

### **MEDIUM**

**Reason:**
- **HIGH confidence** that the failure is a Docker-layer issue on `san-w175-ha1-4-06` — exit code 125 is unambiguous and directly evidenced in CloudBees step metadata.
- **MEDIUM confidence** on the specific sub-cause (ECR pull vs. daemon error vs. volume mount) because:
  - The step log file `Make.f1792e93-a5f7-f1fc-bfff-2686f9881e6f.log` was not retrievable via Splunk or Datadog — it must be read directly from the CloudBees log store.
  - Datadog returned zero log entries for `san-w175-ha1-4-06` during the failure window — Docker daemon logs are unavailable.
  - No ECR-side telemetry is available to confirm a pull failure.
- Confidence cannot be elevated to HIGH without direct step log evidence confirming the Docker error message.

---

## # 9 Infrastructure vs. Code Assessment

| Assessment | Result |
|---|---|
| **Infrastructure Issue** | **YES** |
| **Code Issue** | **NO** |
| **Mixed** | **NO** |

**Reason:**
The failure occurred at the Docker container launch layer — `docker run` returned exit 125 before the build script (`trustzone_images/build/ms/build_all.py`) or compiler was ever invoked. The build script, source code, and compiler were never reached. The `failedReason=Compile` in BuildInfoJson reflects the build phase classification (compile phase), not a compiler or code error. There is no evidence of compiler errors, syntax errors, missing symbols, or test failures. The failure is entirely in the infrastructure layer (Docker daemon / ECR authentication / container runtime) on the agent host.

---

## # 10 Recommended Remediation

1. **Examine the step log immediately**
   - Retrieve `Make.f1792e93-a5f7-f1fc-bfff-2686f9881e6f.log` from the CloudBees log store.
   - Look for the exact Docker error message (e.g., `Error response from daemon`, `no basic auth credentials`, `pull access denied`, `cannot connect to the Docker daemon`).
   - This single action will confirm the precise sub-cause.

2. **If ECR authentication failure is confirmed:**
   - Re-authenticate Docker to ECR on `san-w175-ha1-4-06`: `aws ecr get-login-password --region us-west-2 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 019415958841.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com`
   - Verify `USE_SECRETS_MANAGER=true` is functioning correctly and the Secrets Manager endpoint is reachable from the agent.
   - Check IAM role/instance profile attached to `san-w175-ha1-4-06` has `ecr:GetAuthorizationToken` and `ecr:BatchGetImage` permissions.

3. **If Docker daemon failure is confirmed:**
   - Check Docker daemon status on `san-w175-ha1-4-06`: `sudo systemctl status docker`
   - Restart Docker daemon if crashed: `sudo systemctl restart docker`
   - Review Docker daemon logs: `sudo journalctl -u docker --since "2026-07-06 11:30:00" --until "2026-07-06 12:05:00"`

4. **If volume mount failure is confirmed:**
   - Verify all required mount paths exist and are accessible under user `aswbldsv`.
   - Check `/local/mnt/workspace/CRMBuilds/` mount health and permissions.

5. **Retry the job** after confirming the Docker layer is functional on `san-w175-ha1-4-06`.

---

## # 11 Preventive Actions

1. **Implement ECR token refresh automation**: ECR tokens expire every 12 hours. Implement a cron job or systemd timer on all CRM build agents to refresh ECR credentials every 6 hours. Alternatively, use the Amazon ECR Docker Credential Helper (`docker-credential-ecr-login`) to handle token refresh automatically.

2. **Add Docker daemon health check to CloudBees pre-flight**: Add a pre-step to the `Build.Container.Rel` procedure that validates Docker daemon availability (`docker info`) and ECR connectivity before launching the Make step. Fail fast with a descriptive error rather than consuming 22 minutes of retries.

3. **Enable Docker daemon log forwarding to Datadog/Splunk**: The absence of Docker daemon logs in both Datadog and Splunk significantly hampered this RCA. Configure the Docker daemon's logging driver to forward to a centralized log aggregator on all CRM build agents.

4. **Alert on exit code 125 patterns**: Create a Splunk or Datadog alert that fires when CloudBees step exit code 125 is observed, enabling faster triage of Docker-layer failures.

5. **Monitor ECR pull success rate**: Instrument ECR image pull attempts and failures as a metric. Alert when pull failure rate exceeds threshold for any agent host.

6. **Periodic Docker daemon health monitoring**: Add Docker daemon process monitoring to Datadog for all CRM build agents to detect daemon crashes before they impact jobs.

log_text:
Root Cause Analysis Report
Job ID: f179cbf2-b44b-f18f-b2e3-1ea035bf3243
Generated: 2026-07-07 | Analyst: NOVA (AI Observability Agent)

---
1. 📋 Executive Summary
The CloudBees job CQ2390.LA.1.0-00317-perf.apq.GPS.hy11-2_20260706_232020_232028 failed during the Make step on agent host crm-ubuntu302 with exit code 4 — a GNU Make error indicating one or more compilation sub-processes failed. The CloudBees execution context explicitly classifies the failure reason as "Compile".

Agent host crm-ubuntu302 has zero telemetry coverage in Datadog — no metrics, no logs, no APM spans, and no host inventory entry were found. This means infrastructure-level root cause (CPU, memory, disk, network) on the agent itself cannot be confirmed or excluded from monitoring data alone.

However, the broader CRM Linux build infrastructure (tagged mdb_service:qso.crm-linux-build-infrastructure) shows active disk alerts on /local/mnt on two Hyderabad build hosts (hyd-e160-a05-3-06, hyd-e160-a08-3-03). These are peer build agents in the same infrastructure pool. The most probable root cause is a compile-time failure — either a source code error, missing dependency, or disk/resource exhaustion on the agent — that caused one or more compiler sub-processes to exit non-zero, propagating exit code 4 to GNU Make.

---
2. 🗂️ Job Context
Field	Value
Job ID	f179cbf2-b44b-f18f-b2e3-1ea035bf3243
Job Name	CQ2390.LA.1.0-00317-perf.apq.GPS.hy11-2_20260706_232020_232028
Project	CRMBuilds
Procedure	Build.Rel.602829
Status	completed_error
Outcome	error
Build Name	CQ2390.LA.1.0-00317-perf.apq.GPS.hy11-2
Build Status	Failed
Failure Reason (CloudBees)	Compile
Failed Step	Make (Step ID: f179cbf2-bfbc-f1d8-a05c-2686f9881e6f)
Exit Code	4
Agent Host	crm-ubuntu302
---
3. ⏱️ Build Timeline
Timestamp (UTC)	Event
2026-07-07 06:20:28	Job started (CRMBuilds / Build.Rel.602829)
2026-07-07 06:21:19	Make step started on crm-ubuntu302
2026-07-07 06:21:19 – 06:23:16	⚠️ CORRELATION WINDOW — Make step executing (1m 57s)
2026-07-07 06:23:16	Make step FAILED — exit code 4
2026-07-07 06:24:45	Job ended with status: completed_error
---
4. 🗺️ Execution Path Inventory
Failed Step: Make — GNU Make build invocation
Agent Host: crm-ubuntu302 (Ubuntu Linux, CRM build agent pool)
Commander Host: Not directly identified — no Commander-level evidence found
Invoked Services: Source code (SCM), compiler toolchain, build dependencies — specific URLs/repos not available from metadata
Build Infrastructure Pool: mdb_service:qso.crm-linux-build-infrastructure (peer hosts visible in Datadog)
---
5. 🚨 Detected Error Codes
Code	Source	Meaning	Evidence
Exit Code 4	GNU Make	One or more recipe commands returned a non-zero exit status. Make propagates the highest child exit code (or 2 for its own errors). Exit code 4 indicates a child compiler/linker process failed.	CloudBees step metadata: stepId f179cbf2-bfbc-f1d8-a05c-2686f9881e6f
failedReason: "Compile"	CloudBees executionContext	CloudBees explicitly classified the failure category as a compile failure	executionContext.failedReason field in job metadata
---
6. 🔍 Correlated Observations
CloudBees
Job f179cbf2-b44b-f18f-b2e3-1ea035bf3243 completed with status: completed_error, outcome: error
Step Make ran for 1 minute 57 seconds (06:21:19 → 06:23:16) before failing with exit code 4
CloudBees execution context explicitly sets failedReason: "Compile" — this is a first-party classification from the build system
The build name pattern CQ2390.LA.1.0-00317-perf.apq.GPS.hy11-2 indicates a performance/GPS chipset build for the LA (Linux Android) platform
Agent Host (crm-ubuntu302)
⚠️ Agent host crm-ubuntu302 has NO telemetry coverage in Datadog.
No metrics (CPU, memory, disk, network), no logs, no APM spans, and no host inventory entry were found. Root cause cannot be determined from monitoring data for this specific host. CloudBees step execution logs must be examined directly.

Commander Host
No Commander-specific evidence found. No Commander-related alerts or logs were identified during the correlation window.
Finding: No relevant findings.
Datadog — CRM Build Infrastructure Monitors
The following active alerts were found on the CRM Linux build infrastructure pool (mdb_service:qso.crm-linux-build-infrastructure):

Monitor	Status	Affected Hosts	Relevance
SWENG|ECOMMANDER|BI|PROD - High /local/mnt partition utilization (ID: 139516185)	🔴 ALERT	hyd-e160-a05-3-06, hyd-e160-a08-3-03	Peer build agents in same pool as crm-ubuntu302. /local/mnt is the typical build workspace mount. Disk full on this path would cause compiler write failures.
SWENG|ECOMMANDER|BI|PROD - C-Drive Utilization (ID: 103164699)	⚠️ WARN	crm-windows / hci_windows_build_servers	Windows build agents — OUT_OF_SCOPE for this Linux Make step
SWENG|ECOMMANDER|CLOUDBEE|PROD - ZooKeeper process daemon down (ID: 115792998)	🔴 ALERT	CloudBees Commander cluster	ZooKeeper is used for CloudBees Commander HA coordination. A ZooKeeper outage could affect job scheduling but no direct evidence links this to the Make step failure.
Disk metrics for CRM build infrastructure hosts during the correlation window: The metric query returned data for peer hosts (crm-ubuntu150, crm-ubuntu238, crm-ubuntu152, crm-ubuntutst08, crm-storcon-54, crm-awsdlhyd12) but not for crm-ubuntu302, confirming the agent is not instrumented. No disk saturation was observed on the monitored peer hosts during the window.

Splunk
Splunk was not queried in this investigation as no Splunk index was identified for CloudBees/CRM build logs in the available toolset. CloudBees step logs should be retrieved directly from the CloudBees Commander UI or agent log files.
Finding: No relevant findings from Splunk.
External Dependencies
No SCM, artifact repository, or package manager failures were identified during the correlation window.
The ZooKeeper alert on the CloudBees Commander cluster is noted but cannot be directly linked to the Make step failure without further evidence.
Finding: No confirmed external dependency failures within the correlation window.
---
7. 🎯 Probable Root Cause
Primary: Compile failure during the GNU Make step on agent crm-ubuntu302.

Supporting Evidence:

CloudBees executionContext.failedReason = "Compile" — first-party classification
GNU Make exit code 4 — indicates a child compiler/linker process returned non-zero
Step ran for ~2 minutes before failing — consistent with a partial compile that hit an error mid-build
Active /local/mnt disk full alerts on peer build agents in the same infrastructure pool (hyd-e160-a05-3-06, hyd-e160-a08-3-03) — suggests a pool-wide disk pressure condition that may also affect crm-ubuntu302
Cannot Confirm or Exclude (due to missing telemetry on crm-ubuntu302):
Disk full on /local/mnt on crm-ubuntu302 specifically
OOM (out-of-memory) kill of a compiler process
CPU starvation causing compiler timeout
Source code error (syntax, missing symbol, missing header)
Missing or corrupted build dependency
---
8. 📊 Confidence Level
⚠️ MEDIUM

Reason: The CloudBees metadata directly classifies the failure as "Compile" and exit code 4 is consistent with a compiler sub-process failure. However, the specific cause (code error vs. infrastructure resource exhaustion) cannot be determined because crm-ubuntu302 has no Datadog telemetry coverage. The disk alerts on peer agents provide circumstantial evidence of infrastructure pressure but are not direct evidence on the failing host.

---
9. 🏗️ Infrastructure vs. Code Assessment
Assessment	Verdict	Reason
Infrastructure Issue	POSSIBLE	Peer build agents in the same pool have active /local/mnt disk full alerts. crm-ubuntu302 has no telemetry to confirm or deny disk/memory/CPU issues.
Code Issue	POSSIBLE	CloudBees classifies as "Compile" failure. Exit code 4 from Make is consistent with a compiler error (syntax, missing symbol, missing header, etc.).
Mixed	POSSIBLE	Cannot be ruled out without step execution logs.
---
10. 🔧 Recommended Remediation
Retrieve CloudBees step execution logs immediately — Access the Make step output from the CloudBees Commander UI for job f179cbf2-b44b-f18f-b2e3-1ea035bf3243. Look for compiler error messages (e.g., error:, undefined reference, No space left on device, Killed).
Check disk usage on crm-ubuntu302 — SSH to the agent and run df -h /local/mnt and df -h /. The peer agents hyd-e160-a05-3-06 and hyd-e160-a08-3-03 are already alerting on /local/mnt full — crm-ubuntu302 may be in the same state.
Check for OOM events — Run dmesg | grep -i "oom\|killed" on crm-ubuntu302 to check if the compiler was killed by the OOM killer.
Resolve /local/mnt disk full on alerting build agents — Clean up build artifacts, object files, and intermediate outputs on hyd-e160-a05-3-06 and hyd-e160-a08-3-03 immediately. Apply the same cleanup to crm-ubuntu302.
Investigate ZooKeeper alert — The CloudBees ZooKeeper daemon is down (Monitor ID: 115792998). Restore ZooKeeper to prevent job scheduling instability, though this is not confirmed as the direct cause of this failure.
Retry the build — After resolving disk issues, retry the build on a clean agent to determine if the failure was transient (infrastructure) or persistent (code).
---
11. 🛡️ Preventive Actions
Install Datadog Agent on crm-ubuntu302 — This host has zero observability coverage. Without metrics and logs, future failures on this agent cannot be diagnosed remotely. Deploy the Datadog Agent with system metrics and log collection.
Implement automated build workspace cleanup — Add a pre-build or post-build step to purge stale object files and intermediate artifacts from /local/mnt on all CRM build agents.
Add disk utilization alerting for crm-ubuntu302 — Once the Datadog Agent is deployed, create a disk monitor matching the existing SWENG|ECOMMANDER|BI|PROD - High /local/mnt partition utilization monitor pattern.
Implement build agent health checks — Before dispatching a job to an agent, verify disk space, memory availability, and agent responsiveness.
Stabilize ZooKeeper — Investigate and resolve the root cause of the ZooKeeper daemon outage to prevent CloudBees Commander HA instability.
Enforce build artifact retention policies — Set maximum retention periods for build outputs on /local/mnt to prevent disk exhaustion across the build agent pool.
---
12. 📚 Sources Used
Source Type	Query Window	Evidence	Relevance
CloudBees Job Metadata	N/A (provided)	Job ID, step name, exit code 4, agent host, failedReason=Compile	HIGH — Primary evidence
Datadog Logs (Flex)	06:20:00–06:25:00 UTC	0 results for crm-ubuntu302, job ID, build name	No relevant findings — agent not instrumented
Datadog Host Inventory	Current	crm-ubuntu302 not found in host inventory	Confirms no Datadog Agent installed on agent host
Datadog Metrics	06:18:00–06:25:00 UTC	No CPU/memory/disk data for crm-ubuntu302; peer host data available	Confirms telemetry gap; peer hosts show normal CPU
Datadog Monitors	Current state	Active /local/mnt disk alerts on hyd-e160-a05-3-06, hyd-e160-a08-3-03; ZooKeeper down alert	MEDIUM — Circumstantial infrastructure evidence
Datadog Events	06:18:00–06:25:00 UTC	No events for crm-ubuntu302 or CRMBuilds job	No relevant findings
Datadog Incidents	05:00–07:00 UTC	0 incidents matching crm-ubuntu302 or CRMBuilds	No relevant findings
Datadog APM Spans	06:20:00–06:25:00 UTC	0 spans from crm-ubuntu302	No relevant findings

---

# CloudBees CD/RO — Root Cause Analysis Report

---

## # 1 Summary

Job `CI_TZ.XF.5.25-00179.2-KALPENIAAAAANAZT-2_20260706_021954_022000` (Job ID: `f1791bdd-0bec-f151-8192-1ea035bf3243`) failed on **2026-07-06** at **09:44:05 UTC** due to the `BBS_PackMake` step exiting with **exit code 125** on agent host **crm-ubuntu232**. The CloudBees `errorHandling=abortJob` policy on that step triggered an immediate job abort.

The `BuildInfoJson` property explicitly records `failedReason=PostPackBBS`, confirming the failure occurred during the **post-pack BBS phase** of the build. Exit code 125 in a Docker-based build context indicates the **Docker container failed to run or be created** — not a failure of the container's internal process. This is consistent with a resource-level failure on the agent host.

Datadog telemetry on `crm-ubuntu232` shows **memory utilization rising from ~74% to ~85%** (~42 GB increase) over the job window, with **elevated kernel/system CPU** (cpu_sys exceeding cpu_user during 09:28–09:39), indicating heavy I/O or syscall pressure. The agent's CMDB resource description contains a historical annotation: **"syelugub- 7/31 space issue 7/17 smanley - put back in service"**, confirming this host has a documented prior disk space problem. A concurrent job (`f1791f06-4a84-f1fa-9190-1ea035bf3243`) was also running on the same agent at the time of failure.

The most probable root cause is a **build-time failure within the BBS_PackMake/PostPackBBS phase**, most likely caused by a Docker container creation failure (exit code 125) potentially driven by resource pressure (memory growth, I/O contention) or a Docker-level error on the agent. Direct step execution logs on `crm-ubuntu232` are required to confirm the exact sub-cause.

## # 7 Probable Root Cause

**Primary Root Cause: Docker Container Creation Failure (Exit Code 125) During PostPackBBS Phase**

**Evidence Chain:**

1. `BBS_PackMake` step exited with **exit code 125** — confirmed by three independent CloudBees Commander log events (`FinishCommandOperation`, `RunCommandCallback`, `CommitCommandResultsOperation`) at `09:44:05Z`.
2. Exit code 125 in a Docker-based workflow (`ONPREMCONTAINERIZED`) means **`docker run` failed to create or start the container** — this is a Docker daemon-level error, not a build code error.
3. `BuildInfoJson.failedReason=PostPackBBS` confirms the failure occurred in the **PostPackBBS sub-phase** of the step.
4. Step duration was **~30 seconds** — far too short for a TrustZone firmware pack/make operation to complete or fail on code; consistent with a container startup failure.
5. Agent `crm-ubuntu232` had **memory utilization at ~85%** (~228 GB of 251 GB used) at the time of failure, with a **~42 GB increase** over the job window driven by both this job and a concurrent job (`f1791f06-4a84-f1fa-9190-1ea035bf3243`).
6. **Elevated kernel CPU** (cpu_sys > cpu_user during 09:28–09:39) indicates heavy I/O or syscall pressure on the agent, consistent with Docker image layer operations or filesystem pressure.
7. The agent has a **documented prior disk space issue** (resource description: *"syelugub- 7/31 space issue 7/17 smanley - put back in service"*), indicating this host has a history of resource-related failures.
8. **Direct step execution logs on `crm-ubuntu232`** for the `BBS_PackMake` step are not available in Splunk or Datadog — the exact Docker error message (e.g., "no space left on device", "cannot allocate memory", "image pull failed") cannot be determined without examining the step log file directly.

**Most Likely Sub-Cause (requires log confirmation):**
Docker container creation failed due to one of:
- Insufficient memory to allocate the container (memory at 85%, concurrent job consuming resources)
- Docker image layer extraction failure due to disk pressure on `/local/mnt` (declining free space)
- Docker daemon error triggered by I/O contention (elevated kernel CPU)

---

## # 8 Confidence Level

### **MEDIUM**

**Reason:**

- **HIGH confidence** elements: Job ID, step name, exit code 125, failedReason=PostPackBBS, abort chain, agent identity, step duration — all directly confirmed by Splunk `index=ecommander`.
- **MEDIUM confidence** elements: The link between memory/I/O pressure and the Docker exit code 125 is strongly correlated but not directly proven — the step execution log on `crm-ubuntu232` was not available in Splunk or Datadog.
- **Cannot achieve HIGH confidence** without the `BBS_PackMake` step log file (`BBS_PackMake.f1791bdd-17fb-f111-93af-4efb9ff91010.log`) from `crm-ubuntu232`, which would contain the exact Docker error message.
- No speculation has been introduced. The root cause is bounded by available evidence.

---

## # 9 Infrastructure vs Code Assessment

| Assessment | Result |
|---|---|
| **Infrastructure Issue** | **YES** |
| **Code Issue** | **NO** |
| **Mixed** | **NO** |

**Reason:**

Exit code 125 is a **Docker daemon / container runtime error**, not a compiler, linker, or build script error. The step failed in ~30 seconds — insufficient time for a build code failure. The failure occurred at the container creation level (PostPackBBS phase), before the build command (`python3 trustzone_images/build/ms/build_all.py`) could execute or fail. Memory pressure (~85% utilization) and elevated kernel CPU on `crm-ubuntu232` are consistent with an infrastructure-level resource constraint causing Docker container creation to fail. The agent has a documented prior history of resource-related failures.

## # 10 Recommended Remediation

1. **Retrieve and examine the BBS_PackMake step log immediately:**
   - File: `BBS_PackMake.f1791bdd-17fb-f111-93af-4efb9ff91010.log` on `crm-ubuntu232`
   - Path: `/local/mnt/workspace/CRMBuilds/` or the workspace directory
   - This log will contain the exact Docker error message confirming the sub-cause.

2. **Investigate Docker daemon state on `crm-ubuntu232` at failure time:**
   - Check `/var/log/syslog` and `journalctl -u docker` on `crm-ubuntu232` around `09:43:34Z – 09:44:05Z`.
   - Look for OOM killer events, Docker daemon errors, or storage driver errors.

3. **Address memory pressure on `crm-ubuntu232`:**
   - At failure time, memory was at ~85% (~228 GB of 251 GB). With a concurrent job also running, available headroom was ~22–23 GB.
   - Review the step limit (currently 9) and consider reducing concurrent step assignments to this agent.
   - Investigate whether the concurrent job `f1791f06-4a84-f1fa-9190-1ea035bf3243` should be scheduled on a different agent.

4. **Investigate the `/local/mnt` disk trend:**
   - Free space declined ~31 GB over 47 minutes. While not full at failure time, this trend warrants monitoring.
   - Check Docker image cache size on `/local/mnt` — stale images may be consuming space.
   - Run `docker system prune` on `crm-ubuntu232` to reclaim disk space.

5. **Retry the failed job** after confirming agent resource health.

---

## # 11 Preventive Actions

1. **Implement memory utilization alerting on CRM build agents:**
   - Alert threshold: >80% memory utilization sustained for >5 minutes on `CRMBuildsLinux-Ubuntu20-04` pool agents.
   - This would have flagged `crm-ubuntu232` before the failure.

2. **Implement disk space alerting on `/local/mnt`:**
   - Alert threshold: <200 GB free on `/local/mnt` (currently declining).
   - Given the documented prior space issue on `crm-ubuntu232`, this is a known risk.

3. **Implement Docker health checks as a pre-step:**
   - Add a lightweight pre-flight step before `BBS_PackMake` to verify Docker daemon health and available disk/memory.

4. **Review concurrent step scheduling policy for `crm-ubuntu232`:**
   - The agent ran 2 concurrent jobs at the time of failure. Consider reducing `stepLimit` or implementing resource-aware scheduling to prevent memory contention.

5. **Establish a regular Docker image cache cleanup schedule on all CRM build agents:**
   - Scheduled `docker system prune -f` to prevent disk accumulation on `/local/mnt`.

6. **Document and track the `crm-ubuntu232` resource history:**
   - The existing annotation (*"space issue 7/17 smanley - put back in service"*) indicates this host has recurring resource issues. Consider a formal health review before returning it to the production pool.

1. Executive Summary
The CloudBees CI build CI_BOOT.XF.4.3-00263.1-ATHERTONLAZ-70 failed on 2026-07-07 due to a Compile Failure in the Make step. The Make step executed on agent host san-w175-ha1-4-07 and exited with an error, triggering an abortJob error-handling policy. This cascaded into a SaveBuild step failure and the cancellation of all downstream packaging and post-processing steps. The agent host showed no resource exhaustion (CPU, memory, disk all healthy). The root cause is a source code or build script compile error within the buildex.py-driven UEFI firmware build for the AthertonPkg target (variants LAA/LAB, Ubuntu 16.04).

7. Probable Root Cause
PRIMARY ROOT CAUSE: Compile Failure in the Make Step

The build framework's buildex.py script invoked the UEFI firmware compilation for AthertonPkg (variants LAA/LAB) on agent san-w175-ha1-4-07. The Make step failed after ~2 min 31 sec of execution. The BuildInfoJson property was explicitly set to failedReason: Compile by the build framework, indicating the failure was detected within the compilation phase — not in packaging, artifact storage, or infrastructure.

The agent host had no resource constraints (CPU, memory, disk all healthy). The Commander nodes were healthy. External services were reachable (Setup succeeded). The failure is therefore attributable to a source code or build script error within the BOOT.XF.4.3 branch at revision 00263, spin 70.

Supporting Evidence:

BuildInfoJson: "failedReason":"Compile", "status":"Failed"
CloudBees errorMessage: "step 'Make' failed and its error handling aborted the job"
Make step duration: ~2m31s (partial compile, not a timeout — timeout was set to 5 hours)
Setup step: exitCode 0 — environment was valid
Agent host: No CPU/memory/disk exhaustion
No SCOM alerts on agent or Commander nodes
---
8. Confidence Level
MEDIUM–HIGH
Reason: The CloudBees job metadata and BuildInfoJson explicitly classify the failure as Compile. Infrastructure telemetry confirms no resource exhaustion on the agent. However, the specific compiler error lines (GCC/LLVM output) from the Make step log file (Make.<stepId>.log) were not directly accessible via Splunk, preventing identification of the exact failing source file, function, or error code. Confidence would be HIGH with direct Make step log access.

Infrastructure Issue	NO
Code / Compile Issue	YES
Mixed Issue	NO
Reason: Agent host resources (CPU, memory, disk) were all within healthy ranges. No SCOM alerts. Commander nodes were healthy. The failure is explicitly classified as Compile by the build framework. The Make step ran for ~2.5 minutes before failing — consistent with a source code compilation error encountered mid-build, not an infrastructure timeout or resource exhaustion event.

Examine the Make step log directly: Retrieve Make.f179e25d-*.log from the CloudBees job workspace on san-w175-ha1-4-07 or from the build artifact location at /prj/qct/asw/crmbuilds/snowcone/builds677/PROD/CI_BOOT.XF.4.3-00263.1-ATHERTONLAZ-70. Look for GCC/LLVM/Python error lines.
Identify the failing source file: Search the Make log for patterns like error:, fatal error:, undefined reference, No such file or directory, or Python tracebacks from buildex.py.
Check recent code changes at revision 00263: Review commits to the BOOT.XF.4.3 branch at or near revision 00263 that affect AthertonPkg (LAA/LAB variants). Identify any changes to header files, build scripts, or toolchain configurations.
Verify toolchain availability: Confirm Python 3.8.2 (/pkg/qct/software/ubuntu/python/3.8.2/bin) and all required build tools are present and functional on san-w175-ha1-4-07.
Re-run the build after identifying and fixing the compile error. If the error is in a dependency (QcomToolsPkg, QcomTestPkg), fix the dependency first.
Monitor /usr/vice/cache on san-w175-ha1-4-07 (currently at 72%) — clean up if approaching capacity.

1. 📋 Summary
The CloudBees build Aurora.LA.3.1-00151-STD.PROD-2 failed due to a Compile Error in the Make step. The BuildMongr -makebuild command exited with exit code 4 after only 48 seconds, indicating a hard compile failure. The errorHandling=abortJob policy triggered an immediate job abort, cascading failures to the downstream VerifySrc (forcibly aborted) and SaveBuild (never executed) steps. No infrastructure-level issues were detected on the agent host crm-ubuntu345 or the CloudBees Commander. The root cause is a code/compile-level failure within the build invocation.

6. 🔗 Correlated Observations
☁️ CloudBees
Job f179d493-1daf-f1fc-91e4-4efb9ff91010 confirmed failed with outcome=error, abortStatus=ABORT, failedReason=Compile.
The Make step (stepIndex 10) ran for only 48 seconds before exiting with code 4 — a very short duration for a full build, strongly indicating an early compile-time failure.
errorHandling=abortJob was set on the Make step, which is the direct trigger for the job abort cascade.
The Setup step completed successfully (exit code 0, 15s), confirming the environment was initialized correctly.
All post-failure cleanup steps (GenerateProvenance, Finalize, AddManifest, CleanUp, Record_All_Times, Calculate Metrics) completed successfully with errorHandling=ignore and alwaysRun=true.
🖥️ Agent Host: crm-ubuntu345
Agent is part of the CRMBuildsLinux-Ubuntu24-04 resource pool, PROXY zone, SanDiego site.
Agent version: 2024.12.0.177508 — current and healthy.
Pool activity logs show crm-ubuntu344 had stepCount=2 with stepLimit=9 — pool was not saturated.
A concurrent job (Snapdragon_Auto.HGY.4.1.8.0.r1-00018.02-STD.INT-1) was later observed running successfully on crm-ubuntu345, confirming the agent itself was functional.
No SCOM alerts were found for crm-ubuntu345.
No Datadog logs were found for crm-ubuntu345 during the failure window (no telemetry coverage in Datadog for this host).
No OS-level (Splunk os/osetx index) logs were available for this host during the failure window.
🏛️ Commander Host
No evidence of Commander-side failures contributing to the job failure.
Job scheduling, resource assignment, and step dispatch all functioned normally (Setup step succeeded).
📊 Datadog
No Datadog logs found for host crm-ubuntu345 during the failure window (07:20Z–07:30Z).
No telemetry coverage available in Datadog for this agent host.
📁 Splunk
Splunk ecommander index provided full job metadata, step details, exit codes, and timing — primary evidence source.
No OS/system-level logs (os/osetx indexes) available for crm-ubuntu345 during the failure window.
No BuildMongr-specific error log entries were found beyond the exit code 4 recorded in the CloudBees step metadata.
🔌 External Dependencies
BuildMongr: The build orchestration tool invoked by the Make step. Exit code 4 is the direct failure signal. No external service connectivity issues were detected.
snowcone (build artifact storage): No evidence of storage failures. Artifact path was pre-configured and cleanup steps ran successfully.
LasVegas components: DISPLAY, GRAPHICS, KERNEL, VIDEO, CAMERA, LA.VENDOR were part of the build. No evidence of remote component fetch failures.
---
7. 🎯 Probable Root Cause
The Make step invoked BuildMongr -makebuild Aurora.LA.3.1-00151-STD.PROD-2 and exited with exit code 4 after only 48 seconds. The CloudBees job metadata explicitly records failedReason=Compile, confirming this is a compile-time failure within the build process.

The most probable root cause is a compilation error in one or more source components of the Aurora.LA.3.1 image (spanning SanDiego and LasVegas components). The short 48-second runtime before failure suggests the error occurred early in the compilation phase — possibly a missing symbol, syntax error, incompatible header, or dependency resolution failure within the source tree associated with engineering label GLUE.AURORA_LA.3.0-00040-NOOP_TEST_AURORA-1 at branch point revision 00151.

Supporting Evidence:

Exit code 4 from BuildMongr (non-zero, indicating build failure)
CloudBees metadata: failedReason=Compile
Step duration: only 48 seconds (abnormally short for a full build)
Agent and infrastructure were healthy (Setup succeeded, no SCOM alerts, agent ran other jobs successfully)
Cascade abort of VerifySrc and SaveBuild confirms no further build artifacts were produced
---
8. 📈 Confidence Level
MEDIUM

Reason: The CloudBees metadata explicitly states failedReason=Compile and exit code 4 from BuildMongr, providing strong direct evidence of a compile failure. However, the specific compile error message (e.g., which file, which component, which error) is not available in Splunk or Datadog — the detailed BuildMongr log output would need to be examined directly from the build workspace at /prj/qct/asw/crmbuilds/snowcone/builds907/PROD/Aurora.LA.3.1-00151-STD.PROD-2 to achieve HIGH confidence.

---
9. 🏗️ Infrastructure vs Code Assessment
Infrastructure Issue	NO
Code Issue	YES
Mixed	NO
Reason: The agent host crm-ubuntu345 was healthy (no SCOM alerts, agent ran other jobs successfully, Setup step passed). The failure is explicitly classified as Compile by CloudBees, and exit code 4 from BuildMongr is a build-tool-level failure, not an infrastructure-level signal. No storage, network, DNS, or resource exhaustion indicators were found.

---
10. 🔧 Recommended Remediation
Examine BuildMongr logs directly at the build workspace:
/prj/qct/asw/crmbuilds/snowcone/builds907/PROD/Aurora.LA.3.1-00151-STD.PROD-2
Look for the specific compile error (file, line, error message) before the build delete date of 2026-07-10.
Review recent changes to engineering label GLUE.AURORA_LA.3.0-00040-NOOP_TEST_AURORA-1 at branch point revision 00151 — identify any recent commits that may have introduced a compile-breaking change.
Check component compatibility between SanDiego components (CPUCP, AOP, BTFW, WLAN, TZ, DSP, BOOT, QSSI, GLUE) and LasVegas components (DISPLAY, GRAPHICS, KERNEL, VIDEO, CAMERA, LA.VENDOR) for API/header mismatches.
Re-run the build after identifying and fixing the compile error, or roll back the offending commit.
Contact the build owner (gopu) with the specific error details for code-level remediation.
---
11. 🛡️ Preventive Actions
Pre-submit compile checks: Enforce pre-submit build validation on the GLUE/Aurora component tree to catch compile errors before they reach the full integration build.
BuildMongr log forwarding: Configure BuildMongr to forward detailed compile error logs to Splunk or Datadog for faster RCA without needing direct filesystem access.
Exit code documentation: Document BuildMongr exit codes (especially code 4) in the team's runbook to accelerate triage.
Increase build delete retention: Consider extending the build delete date beyond 3 days for failed builds to allow more time for post-mortem log analysis.
Component integration testing: Add automated compatibility checks between SanDiego and LasVegas component sets before triggering full image builds.
---

log_text:
Root Cause Analysis Report
Job ID: f179cb72-2009-f120-bf77-2686f9881e6f  |  Build: WLAN.TFH.3.6.c2-00721-QCATFSWPZ-2  |  Outcome: ERROR / ABORTED

---
1. Executive Summary
CloudBees job f179cb72-2009-f120-bf77-2686f9881e6f failed during the "VerifySrc" step with exit code 2 on agent host san-w175-ha1-4-07. The CloudBees execution context classifies the failure reason as "Aborted" — the step was externally terminated before natural completion.

This job shares the same step name (VerifySrc), exit code (2), failure reason (Aborted), and procedure (Build.Rel.602829) as the previous job on san-w175-ja4-6-06, but the infrastructure signature is distinctly different:

No large inbound network burst — unlike the previous job's 257MB download, this host received only ~1.2–1.4MB/s during the step. The source fetch pattern is absent.
Massive outbound network burst — 55MB → 40MB → 54MB → 107MB → 98MB sent during the step window (~354MB total outbound in ~77 seconds). This is the dominant infrastructure signal.
IO contention pre-step — IO await peaked at 15.97ms and IO util at 15.97% at ~06:19:23Z, 27 seconds before the step started. This indicates the storage subsystem was already under load from a prior or concurrent operation when VerifySrc began.
Rising system load — load average climbed steadily from 0.81 → 1.97 throughout the window, indicating accumulating process pressure, consistent with concurrent workloads on the agent.
CPU abrupt drop to 0.07% at the end of the step — consistent with process termination (abort signal).
Probable Root Cause: The VerifySrc step was aborted due to a combination of pre-existing IO contention (storage subsystem already stressed before step start) and a large outbound data transfer (~354MB) that saturated the agent's upload bandwidth or triggered a network/transfer timeout. The step likely attempted to upload or push source verification artifacts/results to a remote endpoint. The transfer stalled or exceeded a timeout threshold, triggering an abort. Exit code 2 and "Aborted" classification confirm external termination.

⚠️ No application-level logs are forwarded from san-w175-ha1-4-07 to Datadog. The Datadog Agent is installed and reporting metrics, but no log pipeline is configured. Step-level stdout/stderr must be retrieved directly from CloudBees to confirm the exact abort trigger and the destination of the outbound transfer.

---
2. Job Context
Job ID: f179cb72-2009-f120-bf77-2686f9881e6f
Job Name: WLAN.TFH.3.6.c2-00721-QCATFSWPZ-2_20260706_231638_231652
Project: CRMBuilds
Procedure: Build.Rel.602829
Outcome: completed_error / error
Failed Step: VerifySrc (Step ID: f179cb72-2ade-f1e2-b856-2686f9881e6f)
Exit Code: 2 — script-level abort / explicit exit 2 on detected error condition
Agent Host: san-w175-ha1-4-07 (GNU/Linux, Datadog Agent 7.64.1 ✅)
Build Name: WLAN.TFH.3.6.c2-00721-QCATFSWPZ-2
Declared Failure Reason: Aborted
Build Type: WLAN TFH 3.6.c2 (Thin Firmware Host, older branch) — QCATFSWPZ variant
Agent Rack: san-w175-ha1 — 20 hosts registered in Datadog, all Agent 7.64.1 GNU/Linux
---
3. Build Timeline
Time (UTC)	Event	Detail
2026-07-07 06:16:52	Job Started	CRMBuilds / Build.Rel.602829 initiated on san-w175-ha1-4-07
~2026-07-07 06:19:14–06:19:32	⚡ Pre-Step IO Contention	IO await peaks at 15.97ms, IO util at 15.97%, CPU iowait spikes to 0.129% — storage subsystem under load before VerifySrc starts. Concurrent workload suspected.
~2026-07-07 06:19:11	Large Outbound Transfer Begins	Network bytes_sent: 9.4MB → drops → then massive burst begins. System load starts rising from 0.81.
2026-07-07 06:19:50	VerifySrc Step Started	Step begins — Correlation Window Opens. IO contention already present. Load at ~1.03.
~2026-07-07 06:19:59–06:20:44	⚡ Massive Outbound Burst During Step	bytes_sent: 55MB → 40MB → 54MB → 107MB → 98MB in successive intervals. Total ~354MB outbound. System load rises to 1.97. CPU user peaks at 1.27%.
~2026-07-07 06:20:53–06:21:07	CPU Drops Abruptly	CPU user falls from ~1.02% to 0.07% — process terminated. Load begins declining from 1.97 → 1.55.
2026-07-07 06:21:07	VerifySrc Step ABORTED	Exit Code 2 — step terminated — Correlation Window Closes (duration: ~1m 17s)
2026-07-07 06:25:54	Job Ended	Job terminates with outcome: error (4m 47s after step abort — cleanup/teardown phase)
---
4. Execution Path Inventory
Failed Step: "VerifySrc" — source verification step; on this host, dominated by a large outbound transfer (~354MB sent), suggesting upload of source verification data or artifacts to a remote endpoint
Agent Host: san-w175-ha1-4-07 (GNU/Linux, Datadog Agent 7.64.1 — metrics available, no log pipeline)
Commander Host: Not identified — no evidence of Commander contribution to failure
Invoked Services: Remote endpoint receiving the large outbound transfer (Artifactory, SCM, or build coordination server — specific endpoint not available from metadata); local storage subsystem on san-w175-ha1-4-07
Concurrent Workloads: Rising system load (0.81 → 1.97) and pre-step IO contention strongly suggest at least one other concurrent process running on this agent during the step
---
5. Detected Error Codes & Signals
Code / Signal	Source	Meaning	Evidence
Exit Code 2	CloudBees Step Metadata	Script-level abort — explicit exit 2 on detected error, or return code from a failed transfer utility (e.g., rsync protocol error, scp failure, curl partial transfer). Identical to previous job's exit code.	failedStep.exitCode = "2"
Aborted	CloudBees executionContext	Step terminated before natural completion — timeout, external signal, or explicit abort in script	executionContext.failedReason = "Aborted"
IO Await 15.97ms (pre-step)	Datadog Metrics — san-w175-ha1-4-07	Storage device wait time peaked at 15.97ms at ~06:19:23Z — 27 seconds before step start. Indicates a concurrent workload was already stressing the storage subsystem when VerifySrc began.	system.io.await peak at point 3 (~06:19:23Z), 9s interval series
IO Util 15.97% (pre-step)	Datadog Metrics — san-w175-ha1-4-07	Storage utilization at 15.97% before step start — elevated baseline entering the step	system.io.util peak at point 3 (~06:19:23Z)
Outbound Burst ~354MB	Datadog Metrics — san-w175-ha1-4-07	55MB + 40MB + 54MB + 107MB + 98MB sent in successive ~9s intervals during the step. Total ~354MB outbound in ~45 seconds. Dominant infrastructure signal — no equivalent inbound burst.	system.net.bytes_sent peaks during step window
Rising System Load 0.81→1.97	Datadog Metrics — san-w175-ha1-4-07	Load average rose steadily throughout the window — consistent with accumulating concurrent processes, many in IO-wait or runnable state	system.load.1 rising from 0.81 → 1.97 across 24 data points
CPU Abrupt Drop to 0.07%	Datadog Metrics — san-w175-ha1-4-07	CPU user dropped from ~1.02% to 0.07% at end of step — process terminated (abort signal or script exit)	system.cpu.user sharp decline at final data points
CPU Stolen = 0	Datadog Metrics — san-w175-ha1-4-07	No hypervisor CPU theft — VM-level resource contention is NOT a contributing factor	system.cpu.stolen = 0 throughout
---
6. Correlated Observations
CloudBees
Step "VerifySrc" failed with exit code 2 and failure reason "Aborted" — identical signature to the previous WLAN TFH job on san-w175-ja4-6-06
Step duration: ~1m 17s — slightly longer than the previous job's ~1m 5s, consistent with a larger or slower transfer before abort
Job total duration: ~9m 2s — significantly longer than the step duration, indicating substantial pre-step and post-step activity (setup, cleanup, teardown)
Build type WLAN.TFH.3.6.c2 — older WLAN TFH branch; QCATFSWPZ variant
Confidence: HIGH — directly from job payload
Agent Host (san-w175-ha1-4-07) — KEY FINDINGS
✅ Datadog Agent 7.64.1 installed — full metric telemetry available

IO Subsystem — Pre-Step Contention:

IO await peaked at 15.97ms at ~06:19:23Z — 27 seconds before VerifySrc started. This is a pre-existing IO load, not caused by the VerifySrc step itself.
IO util peaked at 15.97% simultaneously — elevated but not fully saturated
CPU iowait spiked to 0.129% at the same point — processes were blocked on IO before the step began
IO metrics settled to 0.05–3.79% during the step itself — the pre-step IO burst subsided, but the storage device was already in a degraded state entering the step
Contrast with previous job (san-w175-ja4-6-06): That job had IO contention during the step (35.7ms peak) caused by a large inbound download. This job had IO contention before the step from a concurrent workload, with the step itself dominated by outbound traffic.
Network — Dominant Outbound Transfer:

~354MB sent during the step window in 5 successive bursts: 55MB → 40MB → 54MB → 107MB → 98MB
Only ~1.2–1.5MB received during the step — no large source download. This is the opposite pattern from the previous job.
The outbound burst pattern suggests the VerifySrc step was uploading source verification data, build artifacts, or a source tree to a remote endpoint (Artifactory, build server, or SCM)
The transfer was interrupted mid-stream (CPU drops abruptly while outbound traffic was still active) — consistent with a timeout or abort signal during upload
System Load — Concurrent Workload Evidence:

Load average rose from 0.81 → 1.97 across the entire window — a steady, sustained increase
This pattern is characteristic of multiple concurrent processes accumulating on the agent, not a single step's activity
CPU user remained low (0.1–1.27%) while load was high — consistent with processes in IO-wait or runnable-but-blocked state
Memory: 392–393GB usable — no memory pressure whatsoever
Disk: 42.1% used — healthy, no disk exhaustion

7. Probable Root Cause
Primary: Large Outbound Transfer Timeout/Abort During VerifySrc Upload Phase

The VerifySrc step initiated a large outbound transfer (~354MB) to a remote endpoint. The agent's storage subsystem was already under IO contention from a concurrent workload (IO await 15.97ms pre-step). The combination of pre-existing IO pressure and a sustained large upload (~354MB in ~45 seconds) caused the transfer to stall or exceed a timeout threshold. The VerifySrc script detected the failure condition and exited with code 2. CloudBees classified this as "Aborted."

Causal Chain:

A concurrent workload on san-w175-ha1-4-07 creates IO contention (15.97ms await) before VerifySrc starts
VerifySrc step starts at 06:19:50Z with the storage subsystem already stressed
Step initiates a large outbound transfer (~354MB) — likely uploading source verification data or artifacts to a remote endpoint
The upload proceeds but encounters a stall — possibly due to network congestion, remote endpoint slowness, or local IO contention affecting read throughput for the upload
Transfer exceeds a timeout threshold OR the remote endpoint rejects/drops the connection
VerifySrc script detects failure → exits with code 2
CloudBees marks step as failed with reason "Aborted"
Supporting Evidence:

IO await 15.97ms at ~06:19:23Z — pre-step IO contention from concurrent workload (Datadog system.io.await)
~354MB outbound burst during step window (Datadog system.net.bytes_sent)
Only ~1.4MB inbound — no large download, upload is the dominant operation (Datadog system.net.bytes_rcvd)
System load rising 0.81 → 1.97 — concurrent process accumulation (Datadog system.load.1)
CPU abrupt drop to 0.07% — process terminated (Datadog system.cpu.user)
Exit code 2 + failedReason "Aborted" — external termination confirmed (CloudBees metadata)
CPU stolen = 0% — hypervisor contention excluded (Datadog system.cpu.stolen)
Memory 392GB usable, disk 42.1% — resource exhaustion excluded
---
8. Confidence Level
✅ MEDIUM

HIGH confidence that the step was aborted — exit code 2 + "Aborted" + abrupt CPU drop are unambiguous
HIGH confidence that a large outbound transfer (~354MB) occurred during the step — direct metric evidence
HIGH confidence that pre-step IO contention existed from a concurrent workload
MEDIUM confidence on the specific abort trigger — the destination of the outbound transfer and the exact error (timeout, connection reset, remote rejection) cannot be determined without step logs
MEDIUM overall — the causal chain is well-supported by metrics but the final link (what specifically caused the abort) requires step log confirmation
---
9. Infrastructure vs Code Assessment
Assessment	Verdict	Reason
Infrastructure Issue	YES — LIKELY	Pre-step IO contention from concurrent workload, large outbound transfer stall/timeout, and rising system load are all infrastructure-layer signals. The abort was triggered by an infrastructure condition (network/IO), not a code defect.
Code Issue	NO — UNLIKELY	No compiler errors, no syntax errors. "Aborted" classification and exit code 2 indicate external termination. The WLAN source tree itself is not implicated.
Mixed	POSSIBLE	Cannot exclude a remote endpoint issue (Artifactory, SCM server) contributing to the upload failure. The remote endpoint is in the execution path but has no telemetry available.
---
10. Recommended Remediation
Retrieve VerifySrc step logs from CloudBees: Access the CloudBees Commander UI for job f179cb72-2009-f120-bf77-2686f9881e6f → Step "VerifySrc" → View Log. Identify the destination of the ~354MB outbound transfer and the exact error message (timeout, connection reset, upload failure).
Identify the concurrent workload causing pre-step IO contention: Check CloudBees agent configuration for san-w175-ha1-4-07 to determine how many concurrent jobs were running at 06:19:14–06:19:32Z. The IO await spike before the step started indicates another job was performing IO-intensive operations simultaneously.
Investigate the upload endpoint: Once the destination of the outbound transfer is identified from step logs, check the health and availability of that endpoint at 06:19:50–06:21:07Z. A slow or overloaded upload target would explain the transfer stall.
Check network bandwidth on san-w175-ha1-4-07: A ~354MB upload in ~45 seconds (~63 Mbps) is substantial. Verify whether the agent's network interface was saturated or whether the upload was competing with other network traffic from concurrent jobs.
Re-run the build: If the IO contention and upload stall were transient, re-trigger the build. Monitor IO and network metrics during the re-run.
---
11. Preventive Actions
Configure Datadog Log Pipeline on san-w175-ha1-4-07: The Datadog Agent is installed but no logs are forwarded. Configure log collection for CloudBees agent logs and syslog. All 20 san-w175-ha1 hosts appear to lack log pipelines — this should be a fleet-wide remediation.
Add IO Wait and Network Saturation Monitors: Create Datadog monitors on system.io.await (alert >20ms) and system.net.bytes_sent (alert on sustained high outbound) for all san-w175 build agents. Pre-step IO contention would have been detectable before the build failure.
Concurrent Job Throttling: The rising load average (0.81 → 1.97) and pre-step IO contention indicate concurrent jobs competing for storage resources. Implement IO-aware job scheduling — limit concurrent IO-intensive steps on the same agent.
Upload Retry Logic in VerifySrc: Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for the outbound transfer in the VerifySrc script. A single transient network stall should not abort the entire build.
Increase Upload Timeout: If the abort is triggered by a fixed timeout, consider increasing the timeout for large WLAN source tree uploads, or implement chunked transfer with progress monitoring.
Cross-Job Pattern Analysis: This is the second consecutive WLAN TFH VerifySrc abort (exit code 2, "Aborted") across two different agents (san-w175-ja4-6-06 and san-w175-ha1-4-07). Investigate whether the VerifySrc script has a systemic timeout or transfer issue affecting all WLAN TFH builds, or whether the remote upload endpoint is consistently slow/overloaded during this time window.
Standardize Agent Log Forwarding: Audit all san-w175 build agents (both ha1 and ja4 racks) for Datadog log pipeline configuration. Consistent log forwarding across the fleet is essential for rapid RCA.

log_text:
Root Cause Analysis Report
Job ID: f179cbb0-412f-f18b-a4a8-1ea035bf3243  |  Build: CQ2390.LA.1.0-00317-PERF.INT-1  |  Outcome: ERROR

---
1. Executive Summary
CloudBees job f179cbb0-412f-f18b-a4a8-1ea035bf3243 failed during the "Make" step with exit code 4 on agent host crm-ubuntu322. The CloudBees execution context explicitly classifies the failure reason as "Compile". The Make step ran for approximately 2 minutes 25 seconds (06:19:34 → 06:21:59 UTC) before terminating with a non-zero exit code.

Critical Finding: Agent host crm-ubuntu322 has no Datadog telemetry coverage — it is absent from the Datadog host inventory, has no registered Datadog Agent, and produced zero logs, metrics, or events in any monitored data source. Root cause cannot be confirmed from monitoring data alone. The CloudBees step execution logs on the agent must be examined directly.

All other "compile error" and "commander error" log entries found during the correlation window belong to unrelated systems (Gerrit GC operations, GitHub Actions runners) and are explicitly excluded per scope rules.

---
2. Job Context
Job ID: f179cbb0-412f-f18b-a4a8-1ea035bf3243
Job Name: CQ2390.LA.1.0-00317-PERF.INT-1_20260706_231829_231836
Project: CRMBuilds
Procedure: Build.Rel.602829
Outcome: completed_error / error
Failed Step: Make (Step ID: f179cbb0-4c2c-f151-ab07-4efb9ff91010)
Exit Code: 4
Agent Host: crm-ubuntu322
Build Name: CQ2390.LA.1.0-00317-PERF.INT-1
Declared Failure Reason: Compile
---
3. Build Timeline
Time (UTC)	Event	Detail
2026-07-07 06:18:36	Job Started	CRMBuilds / Build.Rel.602829 initiated
2026-07-07 06:19:34	Make Step Started	Step begins on crm-ubuntu322 — Correlation Window Opens
2026-07-07 06:21:59	Make Step FAILED	Exit Code 4 — Correlation Window Closes (duration: ~2m 25s)
2026-07-07 06:24:16	Job Ended	Job terminates with outcome: error
---
4. Execution Path Inventory
Failed Step: "Make" — compile/build step invoking make toolchain
Agent Host: crm-ubuntu322 (Linux Ubuntu — inferred from hostname pattern)
Commander Host: Not identified — no evidence of Commander contribution to failure
Invoked Services: Source code repository (SCM) and build toolchain (make) — specific URLs/endpoints not available from metadata
---
5. Detected Error Codes
Code	Source	Meaning	Evidence
Exit Code 4	CloudBees Step Metadata	GNU Make exit code 4 = build error (compilation failure, missing target, or fatal make error). Distinct from exit code 2 (usage error) or 1 (rule failure).	Provided in job payload — failedStep.exitCode = "4"
Compile	CloudBees executionContext	Build system explicitly classified failure as a compile-type failure	executionContext.failedReason = "Compile"
---
6. Correlated Observations
CloudBees
Job metadata confirms: step "Make" failed with exit code 4 on host crm-ubuntu322
Failure reason explicitly set to "Compile" in execution context
Step duration: ~2 minutes 25 seconds — consistent with a partial compile run before failure
Build name: CQ2390.LA.1.0-00317-PERF.INT-1 — PERF.INT build type (performance integration)
Confidence: HIGH — directly from job payload
Agent Host (crm-ubuntu322)
⚠️ Agent host crm-ubuntu322 has NO telemetry coverage in Datadog.
The host is absent from the Datadog host inventory. No CPU, memory, disk, or network metrics were returned. No logs were found. No Datadog Agent appears to be installed or reporting from this host.

Root cause cannot be determined from monitoring data. CloudBees step execution logs must be examined directly on crm-ubuntu322.

Other CRM Ubuntu agents (crm-ubuntu265, crm-ubuntu238) are registered in Datadog with Agent 7.64.1 — confirming the CRM fleet does use Datadog, but crm-ubuntu322 is not enrolled
Reference metrics on crm-ubuntu238 showed a CPU spike to ~24.7% at the end of the window — this is on a different host and is OUT_OF_SCOPE
Reference metrics on crm-ubuntu265 showed stable low CPU (~0.03–0.24%) — OUT_OF_SCOPE
Commander Host
No Commander host identified in job metadata
No Commander-related errors found in logs during the correlation window
No evidence of Commander contribution to failure — OUT_OF_SCOPE
Datadog
Zero log entries matching job ID, build name, procedure name, or agent hostname
Zero Datadog events for crm-ubuntu322
Zero active incidents during the job execution window
Zero monitors configured for crm-ubuntu322
Finding: No Datadog coverage for the failed agent host
Splunk
Not queried in this investigation (Datadog flex tier searched as primary log source)
Splunk ecommander index may contain CloudBees step logs — recommended as next step
External Dependencies — SCOPE VALIDATION
Gerrit GC errors (e.g., fatal: bad object refs/changes/01/220101/1 on qctaisw_tools/hexagon-sdk-6.2.0-win.git) — found at 06:24:06 UTC, after the failed step ended at 06:21:59 UTC. OUT_OF_SCOPE — no direct link to the Make step, and occurred after failure.
GitHub Actions runner log (ci-foundry, Yocto build) — unrelated CI system, different job ID, different runner. OUT_OF_SCOPE
Gerrit CD sanity checker error (missing cd.xml) — unrelated Android_Main project. OUT_OF_SCOPE
---
7. Probable Root Cause
Primary: Compile Failure during Make step on crm-ubuntu322

The CloudBees execution context explicitly classifies the failure as "Compile". GNU Make exit code 4 indicates a fatal build error — typically a compiler error, missing source file, missing symbol, or unresolvable dependency that caused make to abort. The step ran for ~2m 25s before failing, consistent with a partial compile run that encountered a fatal error mid-build.

Supporting Evidence:

exitCode = 4 (GNU Make fatal error) — from CloudBees job payload
failedReason = "Compile" — from CloudBees executionContext
Step name = "Make" — direct invocation of the make build system
Build type = PERF.INT (performance integration) — may involve specialized compiler flags or toolchains
Cannot confirm specific compile error (missing symbol, syntax error, toolchain issue, missing dependency) without direct access to the Make step's stdout/stderr logs on crm-ubuntu322.

---
8. Confidence Level
⚠️ MEDIUM

The failure category (Compile) is confirmed with HIGH confidence from CloudBees metadata
The specific compile error (root file, line, symbol) is UNKNOWN — no step logs available in any monitored system
Agent host has zero telemetry coverage — infrastructure contribution (disk full, OOM, NFS mount failure) cannot be ruled out or confirmed
Overall confidence is MEDIUM: category is clear, specific cause requires direct log access
---
9. Infrastructure vs Code Assessment
Assessment	Verdict	Reason
Infrastructure Issue	POSSIBLE — UNCONFIRMED	No agent telemetry available. Cannot confirm or exclude disk exhaustion, OOM, or NFS issues on crm-ubuntu322.
Code Issue	LIKELY — YES	CloudBees explicitly classifies as "Compile" failure. Exit code 4 from GNU Make is consistent with a compiler/build error in source code or build scripts.
Mixed	POSSIBLE	Cannot be excluded without agent-level evidence.
---
10. Recommended Remediation
Retrieve Make step logs directly from crm-ubuntu322: Access the CloudBees agent workspace on crm-ubuntu322 and retrieve the stdout/stderr output of the "Make" step. Look for compiler error messages (e.g., error:, undefined reference, No rule to make target, fatal error).
Check CloudBees job log archive: Access the CloudBees Commander UI for job f179cbb0-412f-f18b-a4a8-1ea035bf3243 → Step "Make" → View Log to retrieve the full step output.
Identify the failing compilation unit: The Make exit code 4 typically appears after a compiler error. Identify the specific source file, module, or target that failed.
Check for recent code changes: Review recent commits to the source tree used by build CQ2390.LA.1.0-00317-PERF.INT-1 (branch/tag associated with this PERF.INT build) for changes that may have introduced a compile error.
Verify toolchain availability: Confirm that the compiler, linker, and all required build tools are present and correctly versioned on crm-ubuntu322.
Check disk space on crm-ubuntu322: Verify that the build workspace has sufficient disk space (Make exit code 4 can also occur if the compiler cannot write output files due to disk exhaustion).
---
11. Preventive Actions
Install Datadog Agent on crm-ubuntu322: This host has zero observability coverage. Install and configure the Datadog Agent to collect CPU, memory, disk, and process metrics. This is critical for future RCA investigations.
Enable CloudBees log forwarding to Datadog/Splunk: Configure CloudBees agent step logs to be forwarded to a centralized log platform so that compile errors are searchable without direct host access.
Add disk space monitoring: Implement disk utilization alerts on all CRM build agents to detect disk exhaustion before it causes build failures.
Standardize agent enrollment: Audit all CRM build agents against the Datadog host inventory. Several CRM Ubuntu agents are enrolled (crm-ubuntu265, crm-ubuntu238) but crm-ubuntu322 is not — ensure consistent coverage across the fleet.
Pre-build environment validation: Add a pre-step to the CloudBees procedure to validate toolchain availability, disk space, and required dependencies before invoking Make.
JIRA tracking: A JIRA search for CQ2390 was active during the build window — ensure build failures are automatically linked to JIRA tickets for tracking and trend analysis.
---
12. Sources Used
Source Type	Query Window	Evidence	Relevance
CloudBees Job Payload	N/A (provided)	Job ID, step name, exit code 4, agent host, failure reason "Compile"	HIGH — Primary evidence source
Datadog Logs (Flex)	06:18:00–06:25:00 UTC	No relevant findings for job ID, build name, agent host, or procedure	Searched — no coverage for crm-ubuntu322
Datadog Host Inventory	Current	crm-ubuntu322 NOT present. 20 other CRM hosts found. crm-ubuntu265, crm-ubuntu238 confirmed with Agent 7.64.1	HIGH — Confirms telemetry gap
Datadog Metrics	06:19:00–06:22:30 UTC	No metrics for crm-ubuntu322. Reference metrics retrieved for crm-ubuntu265 and crm-ubuntu238 (different hosts)	No relevant findings for failed host
Datadog Events	06:18:00–06:25:00 UTC	No events for crm-ubuntu322	No relevant findings
Datadog Incidents	06:00:00–06:25:00 UTC	Zero active incidents during job window	No relevant findings
Datadog Monitors	Current	No monitors configured for crm-ubuntu322	Confirms monitoring gap
